steps.title: Er Is... Er Zijn...
steps.desc: 'Er' is the most misunderstood word in Dutch. It has FIVE different uses, and they all look the same. This unit will master them all. We start with the one you already know: existential er (there is/there are).
goals: Position verbs with er: staan, liggen, zitten, hangen

steps.title: 📍 Five Uses of Er — Overview
steps.text:
Er has FIVE uses in Dutch:

1. EXISTENTIAL: Er is een man. (There is a man.)
2. LOCATIVE: Ik woon er. (I live there.)
3. PREPOSITIONAL: Ik denk erover na. (I think about it.)
4. PARTITIVE: Ik heb er drie. (I have three of them.)
5. EXPLETIVE: Er wordt gewerkt. (Work is being done.)

They all use the same word: 'er'.
The meaning changes based on context.

This unit covers ALL FIVE. One per lesson.
By L7 you'll combine them like a pro.

Let's start with #1 — the one you know.

steps.exampleEn: There is a problem.
steps.note:
EXISTENTIAL er. You learned this in Unit 5!
'Er is' = singular. 'Er zijn' = plural.
'Er is een man.' = There is a man.
'Er zijn veel mensen.' = There are many people.

steps.example: Er staat een man bij de deur.
steps.exampleEn: There is a man (standing) at the door.
steps.note:
Position verbs with er!
Dutch specifies HOW something exists:
'Er staat' = standing (bottles, buildings, people).
More vivid than plain 'er is'.

steps.example: Er ligt een boek op tafel.
steps.exampleEn: There is a book (lying) on the table.
steps.note:
'Er ligt' = lying flat (books, papers, cities).
'Er ligt sneeuw.' = There is snow (lying).
'Amsterdam ligt in Noord-Holland.'
= Amsterdam lies in North Holland.

steps.example: Er zit een kat in de tuin.
steps.exampleEn: There is a cat (sitting) in the garden.
steps.note:
'Er zit' = sitting/contained.
'Er zit geld in mijn portemonnee.'
= There is money in my wallet.
Also for stains: 'Er zit een vlek op je shirt.'

steps.example: Er hangt een schilderij aan de muur.
steps.exampleEn: There is a painting (hanging) on the wall.
steps.note:
'Er hangt' = hanging.
'Er hangt een jas aan de kapstok.'
= There's a coat on the coat rack.
Four position verbs: staan, liggen, zitten, hangen.

steps.title: 📐 Er + Position Verbs
steps.text:
Plain: Er IS een boek op tafel.
= There is a book on the table. (neutral)

Better: Er LIGT een boek op tafel.
= There lies a book on the table. (natural Dutch!)

staan → vertical/upright: bottles, buildings, people
liggen → flat/horizontal: books, cities, snow
zitten → contained/enclosed: money in wallet, cat in garden
hangen → suspended: paintings, coats, lamps

Using position verbs instead of 'is/zijn'
makes your Dutch sound much more natural.
This is a B1 skill that natives appreciate!

steps.q: 'Er staat een man bij de deur.' — why 'staat' and not 'is'?

steps.s: Er ___ een boek op tafel. (lying flat)
Er ___ een schilderij aan de muur.
steps.hint: A painting on the wall → hanging

steps.s: Er {1} een kat in de tuin. Er {2} sneeuw op straat.
steps.hint: Cat in garden (contained). Snow on street (flat).

steps.title: Ik Woon Er
steps.desc: Use #2: Locative er. When 'er' replaces a place. 'I live in Amsterdam' → 'I live THERE' → 'Ik woon ER.' The key distinction: er = unstressed (neutral), daar = stressed (emphatic).
goals: er vs daar: when to use which
Position of er in the sentence

steps.example: Ik woon er al vijf jaar.
steps.exampleEn: I've lived there for five years already.
steps.note:
USE #2: LOCATIVE er.
'Ik woon in Amsterdam.' → 'Ik woon er.'
'Er' replaces a place — unstressed.
Like French 'y': 'J'y habite.'

steps.exampleEn: THERE is where I live! Not here!
steps.note:
'Daar' = stressed version of locative er.
'Ik woon er.' = I live there. (neutral)
'Ik woon DAAR.' / 'DAAR woon ik.' = THERE! (emphatic)
Use 'daar' when pointing or contrasting.

steps.title: 📐 Er vs Daar
steps.text:
ER = unstressed, neutral:
Ik woon er graag. = I like living there.
Hij werkt er al jaren. = He's worked there for years.

DAAR = stressed, emphatic, or pointing:
Daar wil ik wonen! = THERE is where I want to live!
Ik ga niet daar, ik ga hier. = Not there, here.

Rule: if you could point while saying 'there', use DAAR.
If 'there' is just background info, use ER.

Position of er:
Main clause: after the conjugated verb.
'Ik woon ER al vijf jaar.'
Sub-clause: before the verb cluster.
'...omdat ik ER al vijf jaar woon.'

steps.s: Ik woon in Amsterdam. → Ik woon ___.

steps.q: When would you use 'daar' instead of 'er'?

steps.s: Ik werk {1} al drie jaar. Maar {2} wil ik niet meer werken!
steps.hint: I've worked there (neutral) 3 years. But THERE (emphatic) I don't want to work!

steps.title: Erover, Ermee, Eraan
steps.desc: Use #3: Prepositional er. Dutch combines 'er' with prepositions to replace 'about it', 'with it', 'on it'. English says 'I'm thinking about it' — Dutch says 'Ik denk erover na'. This is a key B1 structure.

steps.exampleEn: I'm thinking about it.
steps.note:
USE #3: PREPOSITIONAL er.
er + over = erover (about it).
'Denken over' = to think about.
'Ik denk erover na.' = I'm thinking about it.
The 'na' is from 'nadenken' (separable).

steps.exampleEn: I agree with it.
steps.note:
er + mee (= met) = ermee (with it).
'Het eens zijn met' = to agree with.
'Ik ben het ermee eens.' = I agree with it.
'Stoppen ermee' = to stop with it / quit.

steps.exampleEn: I often think of it.
steps.note:
er + aan = eraan (to/on it).
'Denken aan' = to think of.
'Wennen aan' = to get used to.
'Ik moet eraan wennen.' = I have to get used to it.

steps.exampleEn: I count on it.
steps.note:
er + op = erop.
'Rekenen op' = to count on.
'Letten op' = to pay attention to.
'Let erop!' = Pay attention to it!

steps.exampleEn: I pay for it.
steps.note:
er + voor = ervoor.
'Betalen voor' = to pay for.
'Zorgen voor' = to take care of.
'Ik zorg ervoor.' = I'll take care of it.

steps.title: 📐 The Er-Compound System
steps.text:
When a verb uses a preposition + a THING:
→ Replace with er + preposition.

Ik denk over het probleem. → Ik denk erover na.
= I'm thinking about the problem → about it.

Ik ben blij met het resultaat. → Ik ben er blij mee.
= I'm happy with the result → with it.

⚠️ ONLY for THINGS. For PEOPLE, use preposition + hem/haar:
Ik denk aan mijn moeder. → Ik denk aan haar.
(NOT: 'Ik denk eraan' for a person!)

Common compounds:
erover (about it), ermee (with it), eraan (to/on it),
erop (on it), ervoor (for it), erin (in it),
eruit (out of it), ervan (of/from it),
ertegen (against it), erbij (with/at it),
eronder (under it), ertussen (between it)

steps.s: Ik ben het ___ eens. (with it)

steps.q: For PEOPLE, you DON'T use er-compounds. You say:

steps.s: Ik betaal ___. (for it)
Ik denk {1} na. Ik moet {2} wennen.
steps.hint: I think about it. I have to get used to it.

steps.title: Hoeveel? Er Drie!
steps.desc: Use #4: Partitive er. When 'er' replaces a noun in quantity expressions. 'How many apples do you have?' — 'I have three (OF THEM).' In Dutch, that 'of them' is mandatory and it's 'er'.
goals: Use with quantities: er drie, er veel, er geen

steps.example: Hoeveel appels heb je? Ik heb er drie.
steps.exampleEn: How many apples do you have? I have three (of them).
steps.note:
USE #4: PARTITIVE er.
When you mention a quantity WITHOUT the noun.
English: 'I have three.' (no 'of them' needed)
Dutch: 'Ik heb ER drie.' (er is MANDATORY!)

steps.title: 📐 Partitive Er: The Rule
steps.text:
When you drop the noun but keep the number:
→ You MUST add 'er'.

Hoeveel kinderen heb je? = How many kids do you have?
Ik heb er twee. = I have two (of them).

Heb je boeken? = Do you have books?
Ja, ik heb er veel. = Yes, I have many (of them).

Zijn er koekjes? = Are there cookies?
Ja, er zijn er nog drie. = Yes, there are still three.

With 'geen' (none):
Heb je katten? Nee, ik heb er geen.
= Do you have cats? No, I have none (of them).

Position: 'er' goes before the number/quantity.
'Ik heb ER drie.' NOT 'Ik heb drie er.'

In English you can say 'I have three.'
In Dutch you CANNOT say 'Ik heb drie.' (without er!)

steps.q: 'Hoeveel appels heb je?' — 'Ik heb er drie.' Why is 'er' needed?
opts: Dutch requires er when the noun is dropped with a number
steps.ans: Dutch requires er when the noun is dropped with a number
steps.hint: Partitive er is mandatory in Dutch

steps.s: Heb je kinderen? Ja, ik heb ___ twee.

steps.q: 'Ik heb er geen.' — what does this mean?

steps.s: Hoeveel boeken heb je? Ik heb {1} {2}.
steps.hint: How many books? I have five (of them).

steps.s: Zijn er koekjes? Ja, er zijn ___ nog drie.
steps.hint: There are still three OF THEM

steps.title: Er Wordt Gewerkt
steps.desc: Use #5: Expletive/impersonal er. This one appears in passive sentences with no subject: 'Er wordt hier gewerkt.' (Work is being done here.) It's a placeholder — very Dutch, very useful. Full passive comes in Unit 16.

steps.exampleEn: Smoking is not done here. / No smoking here.
steps.note:
USE #5: EXPLETIVE er.
Fills the empty subject position.
'Er wordt gewerkt.' = Work is being done.
No specific person does the action.
Full passive = Unit 16. This is just the preview.

steps.example: In Nederland wordt er veel gefietst.
steps.exampleEn: In the Netherlands, a lot of cycling is done.
steps.note:
Uniquely Dutch! English can't really do this.
'Er wordt gefietst' = cycling happens / people cycle.
'Er wordt gedanst' = dancing is done.
Used for general activities without naming who.

steps.en: work is being done / people are working
steps.exampleEn: People work hard here.
steps.note:
'Er wordt gewerkt' = working is happening.
Used for signs, rules, general statements.
'Er wordt niet gerookt.' = No smoking.
'Er wordt gebeld.' = Someone's calling.

steps.title: 📐 Impersonal Er: The Pattern
steps.text:
Er wordt + past participle.

Er wordt hier gewerkt. = People work here.
Er wordt veel gedanst. = There's a lot of dancing.
Er wordt niet gerookt. = No smoking.
Er wordt Nederlands gesproken. = Dutch is spoken.

This construction:
1. Has no specific subject (who? doesn't matter)
2. Uses 'worden' (passive helper — full lesson in U16)
3. Uses 'er' as a placeholder

English alternatives:
'No smoking' / 'People work here' / 'Dancing happens'

Dutch uses this pattern MUCH more than English.
It's especially common for:
- Rules: Er wordt niet gerend. (No running.)
- Activities: Er wordt gedanst. (There's dancing.)
- Signs: Er wordt gebouwd. (Construction happening.)

steps.hint: Impersonal passive: smoking is not done here

steps.s: In Nederland wordt ___ veel gefietst.

steps.q: 'Er wordt gewerkt' doesn't specify WHO because:
opts: Impersonal passive has no specific subject
steps.ans: Impersonal passive has no specific subject

steps.s: Er ___ hier hard gewerkt.
Er {1} hier niet {2}. Er {3} Nederlands {4}.
steps.hint: No smoking here. Dutch is spoken.

steps.title: Waar... Over?
steps.desc: Question forms of prepositional er. If 'erover' = about it, then 'waarover?' = about what? You can also split them: 'Waar denk je over?' These question forms are everywhere in spoken Dutch.
goals: Split forms: Waar... over? Waar... mee?

steps.exampleEn: What is the article about?
steps.note:
waar + over = waarover (about what?).
'Waarover praat je?' = What are you talking about?
Formal: 'Waarover gaat het?'
Informal: 'Waar gaat het over?'

steps.exampleEn: What can I help you with?
steps.note:
waar + mee (met) = waarmee.
'Waarmee werk je?' = What do you work with?
Formal: 'Waarmee kan ik helpen?'
Informal: 'Waar kan ik je mee helpen?'

steps.exampleEn: What are you thinking of?
steps.note:
waar + aan = waaraan.
'Denken aan' = to think of.
'Waaraan denk je?' = What are you thinking of?
Informal: 'Waar denk je aan?'

steps.exampleEn: What are you waiting for?
steps.note:
waar + op = waarop.
'Wachten op' = to wait for.
'Waarop wacht je?' / 'Waar wacht je op?'
Both are correct. Split = more spoken.

steps.title: 📐 Formal vs Informal Split
steps.text:
FORMAL (written, official):
Waarover gaat het artikel?
= What is the article about?

INFORMAL (spoken, everyday):
Waar gaat het artikel over?
= What's the article about?

Both are grammatically correct!
The split form (waar... over) is more common in speech.
The combined form (waarover) is more common in writing.

More examples:
Waarmee werk je? / Waar werk je mee?
Waaraan denk je? / Waar denk je aan?
Waarop wacht je? / Waar wacht je op?
Waarvoor betaal je? / Waar betaal je voor?

The informal split is what you'll hear most often.

steps.q: 'Waar denk je aan?' is the informal version of:

steps.s: ___ gaat het artikel? (about what)
Waar wacht je ___? (for what — split)
{1} kan ik je helpen? {2} gaat het boek?
steps.hint: With what can I help? What is the book about?

steps.q: The split form 'Waar... over?' is more common in:

steps.title: Er Combineren
steps.desc: The final boss: combining multiple er-types in one sentence. Yes, 'er' can appear twice. 'Er staan er drie.' (There are three of them standing there.) Position rules and combinations.
goals: Position rules for er in sentences
Full er mastery: all 5 types mixed

steps.title: 📐 Er + Er: Double Er
steps.text:
Existential + Partitive can combine:

Er staan er drie op tafel.
= There are three (of them) standing on the table.

First 'er' = existential (there are)
Second 'er' = partitive (of them)

More examples:
Er liggen er twee op de grond.
= There are two (of them) lying on the ground.

Er zijn er veel in Nederland.
= There are many (of them) in the Netherlands.

This sounds odd but is 100% correct and common.
Context always makes the meaning clear.

steps.title: 📐 Position of Er
steps.text:
Main clause:
Er goes AFTER the conjugated verb:
'Ik woon ER graag.' (locative)
'Ik heb ER drie.' (partitive)
'Ik denk EROVER na.' (prepositional)

BUT existential er goes FIRST:
'ER staat een man bij de deur.'

Sub-clause:
Er goes BEFORE the verb cluster:
'...omdat ik ER graag woon.'
'...dat ER een man staat.'

Multiple er's: existential first, others after verb:
'Er staan er drie op tafel.'
  ↑exist.    ↑partitive

steps.q: 'Er staan er drie op tafel.' — the two er's are:

steps.s: ___ liggen ___ twee op de grond.
steps.hint: THERE lie TWO (of them) — Er... er

steps.q: In a main clause, where does locative er go?

steps.s: {1} zijn {2} veel in Nederland. Ik denk {3} na.
steps.hint: There are many of them. I'm thinking about it.

steps.q: Which er-type fills the empty subject in passive sentences?

steps.s: Ik moet ___ wennen. (get used to it)

steps.title: Unit 14 Review
steps.desc: All five uses of 'er' in one review. Can you tell them apart? Let's find out.

steps.q: 'Er staat een man bij de deur.' — which er?

steps.s: Ik woon ___ al vijf jaar. (there — unstressed)
Er ___ hier niet gerookt.
Ik denk {1} na. Ik heb {2} twee.
steps.hint: I think about it. I have two of them.

steps.q: For PEOPLE, instead of er-compounds you use:
steps.hint: Ik denk aan haar (not eraan for people)

steps.s: ___ gaat het artikel? (about what — formal)
Er ___ een boek op tafel. (lying)
